templates.subject: AI is your fastest-growing attack surface — here's how [Customer] can get ahead of it
templates.body:
Hi [First Name],

I wanted to reach out about something we're seeing across almost every customer conversation right now: AI adoption is outpacing security teams' ability to govern it.

The average enterprise now has dozens of AI tools, agents, and integrations in use — and a significant portion of them were adopted by individual teams without IT or security approval. That shadow AI problem is real, and it creates data leakage risks, compliance gaps, and attack paths that traditional security tools simply can't see.

Tenable now has a dedicated solution for this: Tenable AI Exposure. It continuously discovers AI assets across your environment — including sanctioned tools, shadow AI, AI agents, and non-human identities — and ties the risk back to your existing Cyber Exposure Score in Tenable One.

Given what I know about [Customer]'s environment, I think there are a few specific scenarios worth discussing:

- Shadow AI: which tools are your teams using that haven't been reviewed or approved?
- Non-human identities: AI agents that have access to sensitive systems are increasingly being targeted by attackers
- AI governance: building a framework for secure AI adoption before regulators require it

Would you have 20 minutes in the next week or two for a brief conversation? I'd like to show you specifically what we're finding in environments similar to yours.

Best,
[CSM Name]
[Title] | Tenable
templates.notes: Use when AI adoption has come up in conversation or when you know the customer is rolling out AI tools. Works well 30–60 days after a QBR to plant a seed for expansion.

templates.subject: A question about your cloud security coverage — and what Wiz can't see
templates.body:
Hi [First Name],

I know you're using Wiz for cloud security posture management, and from what I've seen it's doing a solid job on the cloud side. I wanted to raise something that comes up frequently when I talk to customers running both Tenable and Wiz.

Wiz is excellent at what it does — but its visibility stops at the cloud boundary. The question I'd ask is: what happens when an attacker compromises a cloud resource and pivots to your on-premises Active Directory? Or to an OT environment? Or to an AI agent with access to sensitive data?

Tenable One is the only platform that shows you that entire attack chain — from a misconfigured cloud role, through your identity infrastructure, to your most critical internal systems — in a single view. That cross-surface attack path analysis is something Wiz architecturally cannot provide.

I'm not suggesting you replace Wiz. I'm suggesting that Wiz alone leaves gaps that attackers actively exploit. Tenable One closes them.

I'd love to run a quick surface audit with you — 30 minutes to map which surfaces you currently have visibility into and where the gaps are. Would that be useful?

Best,
[CSM Name]
[Title] | Tenable
templates.notes: Use when a Tenable VM or Cloud Security customer mentions they also use Wiz, or when Wiz is competing at renewal. Lead with respect for Wiz — don't dismiss it. The pivot is about coverage breadth, not product quality.

templates.subject: Re: consolidating your security tools
templates.body:
Hi [First Name],

Following up on our conversation about tool consolidation — I wanted to share a few thoughts before your next budget review.

The consolidation instinct is right. The average enterprise runs 83 different security tools, and the overhead — licensing, training, integration, alert triage across platforms — is significant. The question is what you consolidate to and whether the consolidation is actually complete.

Most platform pitches consolidate well in one area but create new gaps elsewhere. CrowdStrike is excellent on managed endpoints — but your OT devices, unmanaged assets, and Active Directory aren't covered. Qualys goes deep on IT vulnerability management but has no AD product, no OT tool, and no AI exposure capability.

Tenable One is the only platform that covers all five attack surfaces — IT, cloud, OT, identity, and AI — in a single exposure score. That's the difference between partial consolidation and complete consolidation.

Customers who move from a collection of point tools to Tenable One typically see a 20–35% reduction in total security tooling cost within 12 months, along with a dramatic reduction in the time their team spends correlating data across platforms.

I'd like to walk you through what that consolidation would look like specifically for [Customer]. Can we find 30 minutes this week?

Best,
[CSM Name]
[Title] | Tenable
templates.notes: Use when a customer mentions tool consolidation as a goal, or when a competitor has framed their pitch as a consolidation play. Works especially well in budget season conversations.

templates.subject: One question about [Customer]'s Active Directory security
templates.body:
Hi [First Name],

Quick question — when did [Customer] last audit Active Directory for misconfigurations like unconstrained delegation, Kerberoastable accounts, or stale privileged access?

The reason I ask: over 90% of breaches involve Active Directory at some point in the attack chain. It's the most targeted system in enterprise environments, and it's also the most commonly under-monitored — because most VM tools don't scan it the way they scan endpoints and servers.

Tenable Identity Exposure gives you continuous visibility into AD and Entra ID misconfigurations and attack paths, without requiring agents or elevated privileges. It's read-only, non-invasive, and can be operational within a day.

I'd love to run a quick AD health check demo with your team — I think you'd find it eye-opening. The most common reaction I get is "we had no idea this was exposed."

Would 20 minutes work in the next week or two?

Best,
[CSM Name]
[Title] | Tenable
templates.notes: Works well for any VM or Security Center customer who hasn't yet adopted Identity Exposure. Lead with the question — it creates curiosity without feeling like a pitch. Works especially well after any news about an AD-related breach in the customer's industry.

templates.subject: Is your VM program keeping pace with your environment?
templates.body:
Hi [First Name],

I was reviewing your account ahead of our next QBR and wanted to raise something before we get on the call.

You've built a solid vulnerability management program with Nessus — the scan coverage is strong and your team clearly knows the tool well. But I'm seeing a pattern with customers at your stage: the environment has grown faster than the scanning program.

Cloud instances, remote endpoints, and containerised workloads are being spun up daily — and point-in-time scanning, no matter how thorough, can't keep pace with that velocity. Assets appear and disappear between scan cycles, and the vulnerabilities on them go unseen.

Tenable Vulnerability Management gives you continuous, always-on coverage that automatically discovers and tracks new assets the moment they appear. Paired with VPR prioritisation, your team focuses on the handful of findings that represent real, active risk — rather than working through a list of thousands.

Most customers who make this transition see a 40–60% reduction in MTTR within the first quarter. I'd like to show you what that would look like for your environment specifically.

Could we find 20 minutes before our next QBR to walk through this?

Best,
[CSM Name]
[Title] | Tenable
templates.notes: Classic Nessus-to-VM upgrade play. Use when a Nessus customer's environment has grown significantly or when scan frequency is lower than ideal. Personalise with actual asset count data if available.

templates.subject: Preparing for your renewal — a few things I want to make sure we cover
templates.body:
Hi [First Name],

Your renewal is coming up on [Renewal Date] and I wanted to get ahead of it with you rather than waiting until we're in the final stretch.

Before we get into any commercial conversation, I want to make sure we've done one thing: built a clear picture of the value the program has delivered this year. In my experience, renewals go smoothly when the story is already told — and that story is strongest when it's rooted in your own data.

I'd like to schedule a 30-minute value review before the renewal conversation. We'll pull your metrics from the platform — assets discovered, vulnerabilities found and closed, MTTR improvement, scan coverage — and frame them in the way that's most useful for the conversations you need to have internally.

From there, I also want to make sure we discuss:
- Are there any surfaces in your environment that still aren't covered by the program?
- Has anything changed in your organisation — headcount, cloud footprint, OT, acquisitions — that we should account for?
- Are there products or features you've been meaning to explore but haven't had time to activate?

I'd rather have that conversation now than two weeks before the renewal date. Does [proposed date] work for a 30-minute call?

Best,
[CSM Name]
[Title] | Tenable
templates.notes: Send 90 days before renewal. The goal is to pre-empt the commercial conversation by leading with value. Keeps the renewal from becoming purely a price negotiation. Customise the bullet points based on what you know about the account's specific gaps.

templates.subject: I'm hearing you're evaluating alternatives — can we talk?
templates.body:
Hi [First Name],

I heard through [channel] that [Customer] is taking a broader look at the security tooling stack ahead of your renewal. I wanted to reach out directly rather than wait for a formal process.

I'd genuinely welcome the opportunity to understand what's driving the evaluation. If there are gaps in the program, capability requests we haven't addressed, or commercial concerns — I'd rather hear them directly from you than learn about them at the end of a procurement process.

If the evaluation is really about consolidation or platform comparison, I'd also like the chance to walk through what that looks like with Tenable One in the picture — because the conversation changes significantly when you consider all five surfaces together, not just the products you're currently using.

I'm not asking you to skip the process. I'm asking for 30 minutes to make sure Tenable is being evaluated with full information, and that we have a chance to address anything that might have driven this.

Would you be open to that?

Best,
[CSM Name]
[Title] | Tenable
templates.notes: Use when you get intelligence that a customer is evaluating alternatives. Move fast — within 48 hours of hearing about it. Tone should be confident and direct, not defensive. The goal is to get in the room before the process locks.

templates.subject: Your Q[X] security program results — ready to review
templates.body:
Hi [First Name],

I've put together a summary of [Customer]'s security program performance for Q[X] and I'd like to walk through it with you before we formalise the QBR agenda.

Here are a few of the highlights I'm planning to cover:

- **Exposure Score trend**: [X] points improvement quarter-over-quarter — here's what drove it
- **Assets discovered**: [X] net new assets identified automatically, including [notable finding e.g. cloud instances / shadow IT]
- **Attack paths closed**: [X] critical paths reduced, including [specific example if available]
- **Remediation velocity**: average MTTR on critical findings moved from [X] to [Y] days

I also want to make sure we use the QBR to look forward, not just back. Specifically, I'd like to discuss:
- [Surface or product gap relevant to this customer]
- [Upcoming feature or capability relevant to their priorities]
- Renewal timeline and whether there are any commercial questions to address

Does [proposed date/time] work for a 45-minute session? I'll send a calendar invite with the full agenda once confirmed.

Best,
[CSM Name]
[Title] | Tenable
templates.notes: Pre-QBR outreach to set the agenda and signal you're coming prepared. Fill in the metrics with real data before sending. The bracketed items should be personalised — a generic version of this email loses most of its impact.

templates.subject: 30 days in — making sure you're getting full value from Tenable One
templates.body:
Hi [First Name],

It's been about 30 days since [Customer] went live on Tenable One, and I wanted to check in to make sure the platform is delivering what you expected.

A few things I'd like to confirm are in place:

1. **Asset coverage**: Are all the asset types you expected — endpoints, cloud, OT, identity — showing up correctly in the inventory?
2. **Integrations**: Have you connected Tenable One to your SIEM, ticketing system (Jira/ServiceNow), or other tools? These are often the highest-ROI activations in the first 30 days.
3. **Dashboards**: Is the Cyber Exposure Score dashboard configured for your executive audience? I can help set this up if it hasn't been done yet.
4. **Attack paths**: Have you had a chance to explore the Attack Path Analysis module? Most teams find two or three findings in the first session that they genuinely didn't know about.

I'd also love to understand if there's anything that isn't working the way you expected, or any surfaces that weren't covered in the initial rollout.

Can we find 20 minutes this week for a quick health check call?

Best,
[CSM Name]
[Title] | Tenable
templates.notes: Send at the 30-day mark after go-live. The four numbered questions are designed to surface adoption gaps early — before they become renewal risks. A version of this should also be sent at 60 and 90 days with updated focus areas.

templates.subject: One number your board will ask about — and how to answer it
templates.body:
Hi [First Name],

I've been in a lot of CISO conversations recently where the same question keeps coming up from boards and audit committees: "Are we getting more secure over time, and how do you know?"

It's a deceptively simple question that most security programs can't answer cleanly. Vulnerability counts go up and down. Patch rates fluctuate. The number of critical findings varies by scan cycle. None of it tells a coherent story.

The Tenable One Cyber Exposure Score is built to answer exactly that question. It's a single number — trending over time — that reflects your organisation's overall exposure posture across IT, cloud, OT, identity, and AI. It goes up when you close attack paths and remediate critical findings; it goes down when new exposures emerge. It's the kind of metric that translates directly into board-level language.

If [Customer] is not yet using the Cyber Exposure Score as your primary reporting metric to leadership, I'd like to show you how to set it up and how other CISOs in your industry are using it to frame their risk narrative.

Would a 20-minute call this week be useful?

Best,
[CSM Name]
[Title] | Tenable
templates.notes: Works best for CISOs who are preparing for board presentations or audit committee reviews. The Cyber Exposure Score angle is uniquely Tenable — no competitor has an equivalent single metric across all surfaces.

templates.subject: Do you have full visibility into your OT environment?
templates.body:
Hi [First Name],

I wanted to reach out about something that doesn't always make it onto the security team's radar: the OT and ICS devices on your network that standard IT scanning tools simply can't see.

HVAC controllers, PLCs, HMIs, SCADA systems, smart building infrastructure — these devices are increasingly connected to corporate networks, and they represent a significant blind spot for most security programs. Traditional scanners can't probe them safely, agents can't run on them, and a single misconfigured scan can disrupt operations.

Tenable OT Security takes a completely passive approach — no scanning, no agents, no risk of disruption. It discovers and profiles every OT asset on the network, maps vulnerabilities specific to industrial devices, and feeds that data into the same Tenable One platform you're already using for IT.

The most common reaction I get when customers run their first OT discovery is surprise at how many devices are there — and how many have vulnerabilities that have never been assessed.

Would you be open to a brief conversation about what an OT visibility assessment might look like for [Customer]?

Best,
[CSM Name]
[Title] | Tenable
templates.notes: Use for any customer in manufacturing, energy, utilities, healthcare, or critical infrastructure who has OT/ICS environments but hasn't yet deployed OT Security. Works particularly well when paired with a recent OT-related news event or breach in their industry.

templates.subject: Thinking about CrowdStrike for vulnerability management?
templates.body:
Hi [First Name],

I wanted to reach out because I've heard [Customer] may be evaluating CrowdStrike Falcon Spotlight as part of your endpoint security renewal. I wanted to make sure you have the full picture before that decision is made.

Falcon Spotlight is bundled with Falcon Complete and does provide vulnerability data — but it only sees assets that have a Falcon agent installed. That sounds complete until you account for everything without an agent: network devices, cloud infrastructure without agents, OT/ICS systems, legacy servers, and your Active Directory environment.

In most enterprises, that's 30–40% of the attack surface — and it's often the 30–40% that attackers target specifically because endpoint security doesn't cover it.

Tenable VM scans anything accessible on the network — agent or no agent. And it does so with 220,000+ plugins, the industry's highest detection accuracy, and VPR prioritisation that focuses your team on findings that are actually being exploited right now.

Before any decision is made, I'd like 20 minutes to show you the coverage comparison side by side. I think it will change the conversation.

Best,
[CSM Name]
[Title] | Tenable
templates.notes: Use when CrowdStrike is being considered as a replacement or alternative to Tenable VM. Move quickly — once a bundling conversation starts it's hard to reopen. Lead with the coverage gap rather than product features.

templates.subject: Your AI tools are multiplying — is your security keeping up?
templates.body:
Hi [First Name],

I wanted to reach out about something that's come up in almost every CISO conversation I've had this quarter: the gap between how fast AI tools are being adopted and how fast security teams are getting visibility into them.

The average enterprise added 40+ new AI tools in the past 12 months. Most of them were adopted by individual business units without a formal security review. That means there are AI agents, LLM integrations, and GenAI tools in your environment right now that have access to sensitive data — and your current security stack has no visibility into them.

That's exactly the problem Tenable AI Exposure is built to solve. It continuously discovers every AI asset across your environment — sanctioned tools, shadow AI, AI agents, non-human identities with AI access — and maps the exposure risk back into your existing Tenable One platform.

Three things it surfaces that most customers don't expect to find:
- AI tools with access to sensitive data repositories that were never approved by security
- Non-human identities (service accounts, API keys) used by AI agents that are over-privileged
- Attack paths that lead from an AI agent through to your most critical infrastructure

Given that [Customer] is already using [current Tenable product], activating AI Exposure would extend your existing coverage without adding a new platform or a new vendor relationship.

Would a 20-minute demo make sense? I think you'd find what we surface in your environment surprising.

Best,
[CSM Name]
[Title] | Tenable
templates.notes: Use when a customer hasn't yet activated AI Exposure but is already on Tenable One or VM. Works especially well when AI adoption has come up in a QBR conversation or when the customer has recently announced an AI initiative publicly. The three bullet points are the most compelling — try to personalise at least one with something specific to their industry.

templates.subject: The security question every board is asking about AI — and how to answer it
templates.body:
Hi [First Name],

I've been speaking with a lot of CISOs recently who are facing the same pressure: their board or audit committee is asking pointed questions about AI risk, and the honest answer is that they don't have a clear picture yet.

The challenge isn't that the tools aren't there — it's that AI assets don't behave like traditional IT assets. They appear and disappear quickly, they're adopted outside of IT, they have complex permission relationships, and the risk isn't just about vulnerabilities — it's about data access, governance, and who (or what) has the keys to sensitive systems.

Tenable AI Exposure gives you a board-ready answer to the AI risk question. Specifically, it lets you:

- Show exactly which AI tools exist across your environment and who approved them
- Identify which AI assets have access to sensitive data and whether that access is appropriate
- Map attack paths that lead through AI infrastructure to your most critical systems
- Track your AI exposure score over time — the same format as your Cyber Exposure Score — so you can demonstrate governance maturity to leadership

For [Customer], this would extend your existing Tenable One investment to cover the one surface that's growing fastest in virtually every enterprise environment right now.

I'd like to schedule 30 minutes to walk through what this looks like specifically for your environment. Would [proposed date] work?

Best,
[CSM Name]
[Title] | Tenable
templates.notes: Best used when a customer has board-level pressure around AI governance or when AI has been mentioned as a strategic priority. Pairs well with the Cyber Exposure Score email (em-010). If you know the customer is preparing for a board presentation, offer to help them build the AI risk narrative using Tenable data.

templates.subject: Quick question about your AI agent security
templates.body:
Hi [First Name],

One question — does your team currently have visibility into the AI agents running in your environment and what they have access to?

I ask because this is the fastest-moving gap we're seeing across security programs right now. Most teams have good coverage of endpoints, cloud, and identity — but AI agents are a new class of non-human identity that existing tools weren't built to handle. They act autonomously, they have API access to sensitive systems, and they're often deployed by development or business teams without going through a formal security review.

The risk is specific: an AI agent that's been granted excessive permissions is a persistent, always-on attack surface. If it's compromised, or if it behaves unexpectedly, the blast radius can be significant — and most security teams wouldn't see it until it was too late.

Tenable AI Exposure maps every AI agent in your environment, shows what it has access to, flags over-privileged access, and ties the risk back into the same prioritisation framework you're already using in Tenable One.

This isn't a new platform — it's an extension of what you're already running. Would it be worth 20 minutes to see what it finds in [Customer]'s environment?

Best,
[CSM Name]
[Title] | Tenable
templates.notes: More technical angle — works well for security architects or hands-on security managers rather than CISOs. The single opening question is intentional — it creates a natural conversation opener. Works especially well at customers who have active development teams building AI-powered applications or integrating LLM APIs.

templates.subject: You're covering [X] surfaces — here's what the other ones look like
templates.body:
Hi [First Name],

I've been doing some preparation for our next conversation and wanted to share something before we get on a call.

Based on what [Customer] currently has deployed with Tenable, you have strong visibility into [current surfaces — e.g. IT vulnerability management / cloud security]. That's a meaningful part of your attack surface covered.

But when I map your current coverage against the five surfaces that Tenable One covers, I can see some gaps that are worth discussing:

- [Surface 1 — e.g. Identity / Active Directory]: [brief reason it matters for this customer]
- [Surface 2 — e.g. OT / operational technology]: [brief reason it matters for this customer]
- [Surface 3 — e.g. AI Exposure]: [brief reason it matters for this customer]

The reason this matters isn't just coverage breadth — it's that attackers move across surfaces. A cloud misconfiguration leads to an identity compromise leads to domain admin in Active Directory. If you can see two of those three steps but not the third, you have a visibility gap exactly where you need it most.

Tenable One pulls all five surfaces into a single exposure score and a unified attack path view — so you see the full chain, not just the parts you're already monitoring.

I'd like to spend 30 minutes with you doing a proper surface audit — mapping what you have, what you don't, and what the risk looks like in the gaps. Would [proposed date] work?

Best,
[CSM Name]
[Title] | Tenable
templates.notes: This is the most customisable template in the library — the bracketed sections should all be filled in with real data about the customer before sending. Pull their current product list from Salesforce, identify the uncovered surfaces, and tailor the three bullet points to their specific environment. A generic version of this email is much less effective than a personalised one. Works at any stage of the customer lifecycle.

templates.subject: Moving from point tools to a unified exposure score — what that looks like for [Customer]
templates.body:
Hi [First Name],

I wanted to share a conversation pattern I've been seeing with CISOs who are 12–18 months into a Tenable deployment and starting to think about the next phase of their program.

The first phase is usually about coverage — getting visibility into vulnerabilities across IT, cloud, and identity. Most customers get that right. The harder question comes next: how do you take all of that data and turn it into a coherent story for leadership? How do you show that the program is working?

That's where Tenable One's Exposure Management platform changes the conversation. Instead of presenting a dashboard of vulnerability counts, scan coverage percentages, and MTTR metrics — which are all process metrics — you present a single Cyber Exposure Score that reflects your actual risk posture across every surface, trending over time.

The shift matters because it changes the conversation with your board from "are we patching fast enough?" to "is our organisation becoming more secure?" Those are very different questions, and the second one is the one boards and regulators are increasingly asking.

For [Customer] specifically, moving to Tenable One would also activate:
- Cross-surface attack path analysis — see how exposures across IT, cloud, and identity chain together
- AI Exposure coverage — the fastest-growing new attack surface in most enterprise environments
- Unified asset inventory — a single source of truth across every surface you monitor

I'd like to walk you through what this transition would look like for [Customer] — including what it would cost, what it would replace, and what the ROI story looks like in year one.

Would [proposed date] work for a 30-minute call?

Best,
[CSM Name]
[Title] | Tenable
templates.notes: Works best for customers who are 1–2 years into a Tenable VM or Security Center deployment and are ready to think about the broader platform story. The framing around 'process metrics vs. risk posture' resonates strongly with CISOs who are preparing for board conversations. Pair with the Cyber Exposure Score email (em-010) for a two-touch sequence.

templates.subject: One conversation before your next board presentation
templates.body:
Hi [First Name],

I know you have a board or executive presentation coming up [in Q[X] / next month / soon], and I wanted to reach out before you finalise your materials.

Most security programs present risk to leadership using metrics that are difficult to interpret in a business context — vulnerability counts, patch rates, mean time to remediation. These are meaningful operationally, but they don't answer the question board members are actually asking, which is: are we more or less exposed to a material breach than we were last quarter?

Tenable One's Cyber Exposure Score is built specifically to answer that question. It's a single, trended metric that reflects your organisation's overall exposure posture across IT, cloud, OT, identity, and AI. It goes up when you close attack paths and remediate critical findings. It goes down when new exposures emerge or your environment changes.

I've helped several customers use this as the centrepiece of their board security narrative, and the feedback from boards is consistently that it's the clearest picture of security risk they've ever been shown.

If you're not yet using Tenable One, I'd like to show you what your Exposure Score would look like based on your current environment — and what the transition to the full platform would involve.

Even if this conversation doesn't go anywhere commercially right now, I think seeing the metric will change how you think about reporting security risk to your leadership team.

Would 20 minutes this week be possible?

Best,
[CSM Name]
[Title] | Tenable
templates.notes: Send 4–6 weeks before a customer's known board presentation or annual security review. Works especially well if you know the CISO has been asked to improve their risk reporting to leadership. The offer to show what their Exposure Score would look like — even without a commitment — is a low-friction way to open the Tenable One conversation.

templates.subject: Still trying to reach you — [Customer] renewal on [Renewal Date]
templates.body:
Hi [First Name],

I have reached out a few times over the past [X weeks] and haven't been able to connect. I want to make sure this doesn't fall through the cracks, so I'm sending one more note before your renewal date on [Renewal Date].

I'm not trying to pressure you — I just want to make sure you have everything you need to make an informed decision, and that we have enough time to address any questions or concerns before the deadline.

If the timing isn't right, if something has changed internally, or if there's a specific concern you'd like me to address, I'd rather know now so we can work through it together.

Even a quick reply — even just "not now, try me in [month]" — would be genuinely helpful so I know where things stand.

You can also reach me directly at [phone number] if that's easier than email.

Best,
[CSM Name]
[Title] | Tenable
[Direct phone number]
templates.notes: Use after 3+ unanswered outreach attempts with renewal within 60 days. Keep the tone warm and non-confrontational — the goal is to reopen the conversation, not to pressure. The explicit offer to hear 'not now' lowers the barrier to a reply significantly. Always include a direct phone number on this one.

templates.subject: Last note before [Renewal Date] — [Customer]
templates.body:
Hi [First Name],

I want to be straightforward with you: your Tenable renewal is coming up on [Renewal Date] and I have not been able to reach you or [other contact name] despite several attempts over the past [X weeks].

I don't want your organization to experience a service interruption, and I also don't want the renewal to lapse without you having had a chance to weigh in on it. Both of those outcomes would be bad for your team.

If everything is fine and you intend to renew, I just need [X minutes] to process the paperwork — we can make this very quick.

If something has changed — budget, priorities, internal restructuring, a concern with the product — I'd genuinely like to know. There may be more we can do than you realize, whether that's adjusting the contract structure, deferring certain modules, or working through a specific issue together.

If I don't hear back by [date — 2 weeks before renewal], I will need to escalate internally to make sure your account is covered and there is no disruption to your security program.

I am reachable at [phone number] any time.

Best,
[CSM Name]
[Title] | Tenable
[Direct phone number]
templates.notes: This is the final outreach before escalating internally or looping in the AE and management. Send when renewal is 3–4 weeks out and all previous attempts have failed. The tone is direct and honest without being aggressive — it names the consequence (service interruption, internal escalation) without using it as a threat. The phrase 'there may be more we can do than you realize' is intentional — it signals flexibility without making a commitment. Always CC your AE on this one.

templates.subject: Checking in — is [First Name] still the right person to talk to?
templates.body:
Hi [First Name],

I've sent a few notes your way over the past few weeks without hearing back — I completely understand things get busy, and I don't want to be a nuisance.

I'm wondering if you're still the right person to connect with on the Tenable renewal, or if this has moved to someone else on your team. If so, I'd appreciate a quick introduction so I can make sure we're talking to the right people before [Renewal Date].

If you are still the point of contact and just haven't had a chance to respond, no problem at all — just let me know a time that works and I'll make it easy.

Either way, a one-line reply pointing me in the right direction would be a huge help.

Thanks,
[CSM Name]
[Title] | Tenable
[Direct phone number]
templates.notes: This is the most disarming of the three unresponsive templates — it gives the contact an easy out (pointing you to someone else) and frames the ask as a one-line reply. Works well when you suspect the contact may have changed roles, left the company, or the renewal decision has moved to a different stakeholder. Often gets a response when the more direct emails don't, simply because it requires almost no effort to reply to.
